retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Pathological involvement of the hepatobiliary system in patients with CF typically develops before or during puberty with an incidence of 15% to 17%, clinically manifesting in a diverse manner ranging from asymptomatic elevation in liver function tests to advanced liver disease with decompensation. In infants with CFTR dysfunction, liver dysfunction may manifest in the form of cholestasis, which is relatively rare but is seen in approximately 50% of newborns with CF when it cooccurs with meconium ileus. [17] However, the most common clinical finding in CFLD is hepatomegaly noted on routine physical examination, often but not always concomitant with abnormal liver function tests. Hepatomegaly may be attributable to fatty liver infiltration or focal biliary fibrosis. [6] [18] Common bile duct obstruction and cholecystitis may occur in 1% to 10% of patients presenting with abdominal pain and jaundice. [19] Severe CFLD occurs in 7% of patients with CF characterized by biliary multilobar cirrhosis with or without portal hypertension and has a damaging effect on respiratory function, thereby worsening morbidity and mortality. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: CF is a progressive autosomal recessive disorder that involves multiple organ systems. A child must inherit 1 copy of the CFTR gene mutation from each parent to develop CF. Caucasians have a higher risk of carrying a mutation of the CFTR gene. The life expectancy of individuals with CF has dramatically improved in the last decade and continues to increase with improved treatment protocols. CFLD is now the third leading cause of death, only behind pulmonary and transplant-related complications. CFLD accounts for 5% of deaths in patients with CF. Risk factors for the development of CFD include male sex, meconium ileus, and severe phenotype CF. Until recently, most patients with CFLD presented in childhood; however, recent studies show the second wave of this chronic liver disease becomes evident in adulthood, the etiology of which remains unclear. There is no consensus methodology for diagnosing CFLD, particularly given its variable presentation and patchy nature of the disease. Annual screening for CFLD is recommended for early identification to help prevent or delay its complications and reduce morbidity and mortality. Early identification of progressive CFLD allows monitoring for portal hypertension and prompt management of its complications. LT should be considered in patients with progressive hepatic failure characterized by portal hypertension-related complications, progressive hypoalbuminemia and coagulopathy, severe malnutrition, worsening pulmonary function, and declining quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Differential Diagnosis -- Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is seen in approximately 10% to 20% of the general pediatric population, incorporating a wide spectrum of chronic liver disorders ranging from hepatic steatosis to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) leading to end-stage liver disease. [42] NAFLD, by far, is the most common cause of hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis in children of the Western world. Hepatic steatosis is the most common hepatic manifestation seen in patients with CF, with a prevalence rate of 20% to 60%. Compared to other CFLD manifestations like focal biliary and multinodular cirrhosis, it is not well characterized. [43] [44] The pathophysiology of the development of hepatic steatosis in patients with CF is largely unknown, and long-term prospective studies are needed. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Peliosis Hepatis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Polycystic liver disease Congenital hepatic fibrosis Solitary congenital cysts Hydatid cyst Von Meyenburg complexes Caroli disease (type V choledochal cyst) Type IV choledochal cysts

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cirrhosis is characterized by fibrosis and nodule formation of the liver secondary to chronic injury, leading to alteration of the normal lobular organization of the liver. Various insults can injure the liver, including viral infections, toxins, hereditary conditions, or autoimmune processes. With each injury, the liver initially forms scar tissue (fibrosis) without losing its function. After a chronic injury, most of the liver tissue becomes fibrotic, leading to loss of function and the development of cirrhosis. This activity reviews the causes, evaluation, and management of hepatic cirrhosis and highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care -- Fontan-Associated Liver Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Surveillance biopsies have shown that nearly 100% of patients who have undergone the Fontan procedure develop clinically silent fibrosis by adolescence. Over time, an increasing number of these patients require combined heart-liver transplantation due to advanced liver disease, which may include conditions such as bridging fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing advanced fibrosis in Fontan-associated liver disease. However, concerns about potential complications, such as bleeding, may discourage routine use. In this context, MRI elastography emerges as a noninvasive method for measuring liver stiffness, which can be valuable for assessing and guiding management of these cases. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography plays a significant role in diagnosing CFLD. It should be considered early as it is more sensitive in detecting hepatic steatosis, focal biliary fibrosis, multilobular cirrhosis, and biliary duct defects. However, a normal ultrasound examination does not translate as a low risk for the development of fibrosis, as patients with CF with normal ultrasound examinations have been shown to progress to cirrhosis. [21] [16] Other radiologic tests like CT and MRI are valuable in differentiating fibrosis from steatosis and detecting biliary ductal abnormalities. Although not well studied in CFLD, vibration-controlled transient elastography (VCTE) is emerging as a valuable, noninvasive, inexpensive test in determining the stage of liver fibrosis. [20] The utilization of noninvasive serum biomarkers like aspartate aminotransferase to platelet ratio index (APRI) and FIB-4 index in conjunction with transient elastography can be considered in determining liver fibrosis. [20] [22] [23] A recent prospective study employing supersonic shear-wave elastography (SSWE) in combination with APRI showed good diagnostic accuracy in detecting CFLD in children with CF. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive disorder that commonly affects the White population, with an annual incidence of approximately 1 in 3,500 live births. This multisystem disorder is characterized by genetic mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene on chromosome 7, which encrypts a protein essential for regulating transmembrane chloride reabsorption. This activity focuses mainly on diagnosing, evaluating, and managing patients with cystic fibrosis and liver disease. It highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Once a diagnosis of ARPKD is made, involvement of the liver should be assumed. The initial hepatic ultrasound is often routine or may show only minimal cysts. A baseline hepatic MRI can be considered. Liver chemistries are usually normal in neonates, as hepatic function is generally preserved early in life. Liver function can be followed by laboratory testing (liver enzymes, bilirubin, coagulation studies, serum albumin, and platelet count), periodic hepatic ultrasound, and MRI imaging. The first signs of congenital hepatic fibrosis are usually portal hypertension and splenomegaly, which may manifest as thrombocytopenia or gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding. [12] Consensus guidelines suggest further surveillance imaging as follows: Liver and spleen ultrasound with Doppler of the portal and splenic veins monitoring for intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation. Annual laboratory tests for complete blood counts (especially platelets) should be followed to assess for splenomegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Caroli Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Caroli disease is a rare, congenital condition characterized by segmental dilation of large, intrahepatic bile ducts, which appear as cysts on imaging studies and histopathological examination. This condition is included in a group of congenital disorders known as fibropolycystic liver diseases, which originate from ductal plate malformations during embryological development. Patients with Caroli disease may frequently experience complications such as hepatolithiasis and recurrent cholangitis. Additionally, the patients are at increased risk for cholangiocarcinoma due to bile stasis and chronic inflammation. When Caroli disease is associated with congenital hepatic fibrosis, it is referred to as Caroli syndrome. Unlike Caroli disease alone, Caroli syndrome is characterized by sequela of hepatic fibrosis and portal hypertension. Recognizing Caroli disease and related conditions is crucial for effectively managing and preventing long-term complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pathophysiology of cystic fibrosis and liver disease. Assess the typical presentation of a patient with cystic fibrosis and liver disease. Evaluate the management of the patient with cystic fibrosis and liver disease and its complications. Communicate the importance of an interprofessional team approach and closed-loop communication by primary care providers, hepatologists, pulmonologists, nutritionists, radiologists, and surgeons to achieve optimal patient results. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of CFLD remains a challenge due to the non-availability of a sensitive and specific test. Most hepatic manifestations are not seen until the development of cirrhosis and portal hypertension. Nevertheless, the early diagnosis of CFLD relies on comprehensive clinical assessments, biochemical tests, and imaging modalities (see Image. Periapical Chest Radiograph, Cystic Fibrosis). [13] Approximately 40% to 50% of patients with CF demonstrate intermittent elevations in serum transaminases without any symptoms and are not predictive of underlying fibrosis. Persistent elevation in serum transaminases is uncommon and should be extensively evaluated to rule out acute/chronic viral hepatitis, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, autoimmune hepatitis, celiac disease, Wilson disease, and hemochromatosis. Considering CFLD is predominantly a cholestatic liver disorder, other diseases with similar etiology, like primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), and choledocholithiasis should be excluded. [13] Newer noninvasive tests that employ serum biomarkers like aspartate aminotransferase to platelet ratio index (APRI) and Fibrosis-4 (FIB-4) index should be considered early to determine liver fibrosis. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an autosomal recessive disorder that commonly affects the White population, with an annual incidence of approximately 1 in 3,500 live births. [1] [2] [3] This multisystem disorder is characterized by genetic mutations in the CF transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene on chromosome 7, which encrypts a protein essential for regulating transmembrane chloride reabsorption. [1] [3] CFTR, a chloride channel, is expressed in secretory epithelial cells lining the airways, digestive system, reproductive system, and skin. [2] Homozygous mutations in the CFTR gene impair the transport of chloride ions and the movement of water into and out of cells, resulting in the inspissation of secretions and leading to organ dysfunction. Although CF commonly affects the airways, approximately 10% to 15% of patients with CF demonstrate cystic fibrosis-associated liver disease (CFLD). [4] The improvement in the diagnostic modalities and management of CF has positively impacted the life expectancy of patients with CF. Consequently, there has been a significant emergence of liver dysfunction in CF, complicating the clinical course of the disease. [5] [1] CFTR dysfunction has a significant effect on cholangiocyte function causing an alteration in the final bile composition that causes chronic damage to the biliary epithelium, resulting in the development of a broad spectrum of hepatobiliary complications ranging from cholestasis, progressive periportal fibrosis, biliary obstruction causing focal biliary cirrhosis which may progress to multinodular cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and hepatic decompensation. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Transplantation -- Indications -- Model for End-Stage Liver Disease
retrieved_docs.content: A strong indication for LT evaluation arises when individuals with cirrhosis develop complications such as ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, or hepatocellular dysfunction, typically reflected by a MELD score 15 or greater. However, MELD has known limitations, as it does not account for complications like refractory ascites, recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding, chronic encephalopathy, hepatopulmonary syndrome, portopulmonary hypertension, or severe pruritus—all of which significantly increase mortality risk. To address these gaps, MELD exception points are granted for conditions such as hepatocellular carcinoma, Budd-Chiari syndrome, familial amyloidotic polyneuropathy, cystic fibrosis, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, polycystic liver disease, primary hyperoxaluria, recurrent cholangitis, rare metabolic diseases, and certain malignancies, including cholangiocarcinoma, to ensure fair access to transplantation. [16] Additionally, newer scoring systems—including MELD-Na, integrated MELD, delta MELD, and the recently developed MELD 3.0 (which incorporates sex, serum albumin, and reweighted variables)—aim to improve prognostic accuracy, particularly for women and patients with lower MELD scores. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis and Liver Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: CFLD, as a disease entity itself, is a complication of CF. Approximately 5% to 10% of patients with CF develop multilobar cirrhosis during the first decade of life, and it remains the single most important non-pulmonary cause of death, accounting for 2.5% of overall CF mortality. [9] [47] Most patients develop signs of portal hypertension during their second decade of life and primarily present with upper GI bleeding secondary from esophageal varices. [12] [48] Other common complications of decompensated cirrhosis are the development of ascites, which is considered to be a poor prognostic sign, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatopulmonary syndrome, portopulmonary syndrome, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Progressive Familial Intrahepatic Cholestasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with PFIC will develop end-stage liver disease with significant fibrosis by adulthood. Given the progressive nature of this condition, if patients do not undergo a liver transplant, there is high morbidity and mortality associated with it.

The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified under the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” (other esophageal disorders). Within this classification, the SMQ titled “الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد” (liver failure, hepatic fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other conditions associated with liver damage) is listed as a member of the esophageal disorders group. Although liver pathology is not a primary manifestation of esophageal disease, this coding framework groups these hepatic conditions as members of the broader esophageal disorder category, likely to capture related comorbidities or overlapping clinical presentations. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians and medical coders document and manage patients who present with both esophageal and liver abnormalities in an integrated manner.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: to liver failure, cirrhosis, and other liver‑damage conditions (الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد). Alcohol‑related liver disease is a key etiologic factor for both esophageal and gastric varices, thereby increasing the risk of variceal hemorrhage and its sequelae. Correction of esophageal atresia (Correction of esophageal atresia) further illustrates the clinical relevance of operative management within the spectrum of esophageal disorders. Collectively, the interrelationships among vascular morphology, alcohol‑induced liver disease, variceal formation, hemorrhagic complications, anemia, and targeted esophageal interventions underscore the necessity of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: includes upper gastrointestinal X‑ray, while other non‑OR upper GI therapeutic procedures encompass a range of endoscopic and radiologic interventions. Overall, the knowledge base integrates congenital esophageal repair, endoscopic dilatation, variceal management, lower‑extremity venous disease, and their common etiologic factors (alcohol, liver disease) to guide comprehensive patient care.

Both تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption) and تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis) are clinically associated with esophageal disorders and with دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة. Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is further defined as a child of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (liver cirrhosis), establishing alcoholic cirrhosis as a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis. The SMQ group الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis and other liver‑damage conditions) is listed as a member of both esophageal disorders and دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, highlighting the close relationship between severe liver disease and esophageal pathology. Liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي) is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء (esophageal variceal bleeding), underscoring the risk of bleeding complications in patients with advanced hepatic disease. Overall, the knowledge base connects

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: and lysis of peritoneal adhesions are indirect interventions on the peritoneum, a site of findings such as “فتق البطن” (abdominal hernia) and “اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ” (biliary disorder). Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected roles of abdominal and retroperitoneal anatomy, liver pathology, toxic substance exposure, detoxification programs, and related diagnostic and therapeutic procedures in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, esophageal disorders are associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption) and تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic cirrhosis), highlighting the role of alcohol use in the development or exacerbation of esophageal disease. Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) further encompasses تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic cirrhosis) as a child concept, illustrating the progression from alcohol‑related liver injury to generalized cirrhosis. Hepatic cirrhosis itself is clinically associated with نزيف دَوالِي المريء, indicating that advanced liver disease can predispose patients to esophageal variceal bleeding. The SMQ entity الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other liver‑damage conditions) is listed as a member of esophageal disorders and also as a member of دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, underscoring the interconnected nature of liver pathology with both esophageal and gastrointestinal disease processes. Finally,

retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for managing diabetes during pregnancy, acute cholestasis of the liver, and fetal distress syndrome, indicating its relevance across these distinct obstetric and hepatic conditions and its role in guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2891
retrieved_docs.content: Diet therapy is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for a range of conditions, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red arterioles), انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد (extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction), ورم عضلي أملس (smooth muscle tumor), and nervous system congenital anomalies. In each of these disorders, dietary modification is allowed as a qualified intervention to support clinical management and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 180

Within esophageal disorders, Varices van overige lokalisaties (a child of esophageal disorders) is linked to intestinal varices (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة) and has a child entity of esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) and alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) are clinically associated with hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي) and the broader spectrum of liver failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis and other liver‑damage conditions (الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبِد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ)). These liver diseases predispose to esophageal varices and subsequent variceal bleeding. Therapeutic management of esophageal disorders includes esophageal dilatation (esophageal dilatation) performed directly on the esophagus and classified under both “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء” and “عملية في المريء”. Child procedures within this group are esophageal variceal ligation (ربط دوالي المريء) and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal findings on the chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In turn, alcoholic cirrhosis encompasses hepatic steatosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِري) as a related hepatic manifestation. This relationship underscores that radiographic abnormalities of the thorax can reflect underlying alcohol‑related liver disease, and that the progression of alcoholic liver injury often includes fatty infiltration of the liver (hepatic steatosis) before or alongside the development of cirrhosis. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in linking chest imaging findings to potential liver pathology and in monitoring the spectrum of alcohol‑induced hepatic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2406
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول) is clinically associated with gastrointestinal disorders (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة). The group comprising hepatic failure, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and other liver‑damage conditions (الفَشَل الكَبِديّ، والتَلَيُّف وتَشَمُّعُ الكَبد والحالات الأخرى المرتبطة بتلف الكبد (SMQ)) is classified as a member of gastrointestinal disorders (دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة). Consequently, liver disease manifestations such as hepatic failure, fibrosis, and cirrhosis are considered part of the broader spectrum of gastrointestinal pathology, and alcohol use is a clinical factor linked to these disorders.

Varices of other locations are a subgroup of esophageal disorders and a child of دَوالِي مَعِدِيّة, encompassing esophageal varices without bleeding (دوالي مريئية بدون ذكر نزيف) and esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء), which directly relates to esophageal disorders. Upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) includes gastrointestinal hemorrhage and is frequently associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية), reflecting the clinical link between portal‑hypertension‑related varices and severe upper GI bleeding.
